questions.stem: A patient suddenly develops facial droop and slurred speech. What should the nurse do first?

options.text: Activate urgent stroke-focused assessment and response
Reassess in one hour
Ask the patient to walk to the bathroom first
Offer water and observe

questions.rationale: Acute focal neurologic deficits suggest possible stroke and require immediate assessment and escalation.

distractorRationales.b: Selecting Reassess in one hour would be unsafe, instead of responding to New focal neurologic change is time-sensitive. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The nurse should prioritize Activate urgent stroke-focused assessment and response; otherwise neurologic deterioration remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.c: The problem with Ask the patient to walk to the bathroom first is that it moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, while the decisive cue is Acute focal neurologic deficits suggest possible stroke and require immediate assessment and escalation. The safer answer is Activate urgent stroke-focused assessment and response because delaying neurologic safety assessment can worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.d: Offer water and observe fits a lower-risk interpretation, while the decisive cue is New focal neurologic change is time-sensitive. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The priority is Activate urgent stroke-focused assessment and response, not a step that delays stabilization of neurologic deterioration.

questions.takeaway: New focal neurologic change is time-sensitive.

questions.stem: A patient with asthma initially improves after treatment, but later becomes quieter, less able to speak, and more fatigued. What is the best interpretation?

options.text: The patient may be tiring and failing to sustain respiratory effort
The quieter breathing proves the patient is improving
Fatigue is unrelated if the lungs were wheezing earlier
The nurse can wait until the next round to reassess

questions.rationale: A quieter patient who is more fatigued can actually be worsening. Less noise is not always better when work of breathing is falling off because the patient is tiring. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Less sound can mean less effort, not improvement. - C Earlier wheezing does not rule out later fatigue. - D This pattern needs prompt reassessment.

distractorRationales.b: The clinical error in The quieter breathing proves the patient is improving is that it moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, and misses that A patient with asthma initially improves after treatment. It is less defensible because The patient may be tiring and failing to sustain respiratory effort targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.c: This choice focuses on Fatigue is unrelated if the lungs were wheezing earlier, although the nurse should be guided by A patient with asthma initially improves after treatment. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The priority is The patient may be tiring and failing to sustain respiratory effort, not a step that delays stabilization of oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Selecting The nurse can wait until the next round to reassess would be unsafe, instead of responding to A quieter patient who is more fatigued can actually be worsening. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The priority is The patient may be tiring and failing to sustain respiratory effort, not a step that delays stabilization of oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Less audible effort can be a bad sign if the patient is tiring.

questions.stem: A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision, and seeing halos around lights. Which interpretation by the nurse is most accurate?

options.text: The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation.
This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue.
The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home.
These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well.

questions.rationale: Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency that can threaten vision. The nurse should recognize the need for urgent ophthalmic evaluation rather than dismissing the symptoms as routine strain. Time-sensitive treatment matters for outcome.

distractorRationales.b: The reasoning trap in This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue is that it treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, when the bedside priority is driven by Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency. The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.c: This choice focuses on The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home, instead of responding to A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so Choosing it can delay bedside safety action and leave clinical safety risk unresolved.
distractorRationales.d: Selecting These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well would be unsafe, despite the clinical warning in A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of clinical safety risk and postpones the needed nursing response.

questions.takeaway: Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma.

questions.stem: A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg, and sodium 128 mEq/L. Which provider prescription should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids.
Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight.
Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn.
Teach the client about low-sodium foods.

questions.rationale: Hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting, and weakness in a client with adrenal insufficiency suggest adrenal crisis. The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse. Delaying treatment for further testing can be life-threatening.

distractorRationales.b: Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight is not the best answer, when the bedside priority is driven by The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.c: Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn would not address the priority, without accounting for sodium 128 mEq/L. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids is safer because it addresses renal injury before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: The problem with Teach the client about low-sodium foods is that it moves to teaching or reassurance before the immediate risk is stabilized, while the decisive cue is A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg. Choosing it can delay renal protection and leave renal injury unresolved.

questions.takeaway: Suspected adrenal crisis requires steroids and isotonic fluids right away.

questions.stem: A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives, and swelling of the lips. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine.
Obtain a full allergy history before intervening.
Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction.
Document the event and continue monitoring.

questions.rationale: Wheezing, hives, and lip swelling indicate anaphylaxis. The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise and shock can develop quickly. Delaying definitive treatment can be fatal.

distractorRationales.b: Selecting Obtain a full allergy history before intervening would be unsafe, while the decisive cue is A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of oxygenation or ventilation and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: This choice focuses on Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction, while the decisive cue is A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay airway and breathing intervention and leave oxygenation or ventilation unresolved.
distractorRationales.d: Document the event and continue monitoring would not address the priority, even though the higher-risk finding is The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The correct path is Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.

questions.takeaway: When anaphylaxis begins, stop the trigger and act for airway and circulation immediately.

questions.stem: A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure of 208/110 mmHg. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention.
Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature.
Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes.
Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered by bladder distention. The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away. Delay increases the risk of stroke or seizure.

distractorRationales.b: This option overweights Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature, despite the clinical warning in The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay neurologic safety assessment and leave neurologic deterioration unresolved.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor pulls attention toward Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes, when the bedside priority is driven by Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.d: This choice focuses on Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes, rather than acting on A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of neurologic deterioration and postpones the needed nursing response.

questions.takeaway: Autonomic dysreflexia is a remove-the-trigger emergency, often starting with sitting the client up and checking the bladder.

questions.stem: A nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia. Fifteen minutes later the client reports chills, low back pain, and chest tightness. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline.
Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes.
Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion.
Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response.

questions.rationale: Chills, low back pain, and chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction. The nurse should stop the blood immediately to prevent further exposure and maintain IV access with normal saline using new tubing so emergency treatment can be given. Slowing the infusion or masking symptoms delays the response to a potentially life-threatening complication.

distractorRationales.b: This choice focuses on Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes, rather than acting on A nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of circulation or perfusion and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: The reasoning trap in Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, when the bedside priority is driven by chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction. Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.d: Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response fits a lower-risk interpretation, instead of responding to Chills, low back pain. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so Choosing it can delay circulation and perfusion support and leave circulation or perfusion unresolved.

questions.takeaway: Suspected transfusion reaction means stop the blood first and keep the line open with normal saline.

questions.stem: A client with profuse diarrhea tests positive for Clostridioides difficile. Which infection-control action should the nurse implement?

options.text: Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves.
Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster.
Place the client on droplet precautions only.
Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel.

questions.rationale: C. difficile spores are not reliably removed by alcohol-based hand rub alone, so soap-and-water hand hygiene is required after glove removal. Contact precautions and dedicated equipment are also important. Using only sanitizer or shared equipment increases transmission risk.

distractorRationales.b: This option makes Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster the focus, but the stem cue is c diff spore precautions infection. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so It is less defensible because Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.c: This answer choice treats Place the client on droplet precautions only as the key issue, while the decisive cue is c diff spore precautions infection. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves; otherwise infection progression remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor pulls attention toward Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel, when the bedside priority is driven by c diff spore precautions infection. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.

questions.takeaway: C. difficile care requires soap-and-water hand hygiene and contact precautions.

questions.stem: Which action by the nurse best helps prevent a central line-associated bloodstream infection in a hospitalized client?

options.text: Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing.
Change the dressing only when the client reports discomfort.
Use the line for routine blood draws to avoid extra venipunctures.
Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions.

questions.rationale: Consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections. Using the line casually or leaving it uncapped increases contamination risk. Dressing changes should follow evidence-based timing and contamination rules, not patient comfort alone.

distractorRationales.b: This option overweights Change the dressing only when the client reports discomfort, rather than acting on Preventing CLABSI depends on aseptic access and sterile site care every time. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of infection progression and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: This option makes Use the line for routine blood draws to avoid extra venipunctures the focus, but the stem cue is Consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.d: Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions fits a lower-risk interpretation, while the decisive cue is Consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of infection progression and postpones the needed nursing response.

questions.takeaway: Preventing CLABSI depends on aseptic access and sterile site care every time.

questions.stem: A nurse caring for a client with a chest tube notes continuous bubbling in the water-seal chamber. What is the nurse's priority action?

options.text: Check the tubing and drainage system connections for an air leak.
Clamp the chest tube near the client's chest for 30 minutes.
Add more sterile water to the suction-control chamber.
Strip the tubing toward the drainage system.

questions.rationale: Continuous bubbling in the water-seal chamber suggests an air leak somewhere in the system. The nurse should first assess connections and tubing to locate the leak while keeping the system functional. Prolonged clamping can cause a tension pneumothorax, and stripping the tubing is not recommended because it can increase intrathoracic pressure.

distractorRationales.b: This answer choice treats Clamp the chest tube near the client's chest for 30 minutes as the key issue, although the nurse should be guided by The nurse should first assess connections and tubing to locate the leak while keeping the system functional. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The safer answer is Check the tubing and drainage system connections for an air leak because delaying circulation and perfusion support can worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: This choice focuses on Add more sterile water to the suction-control chamber, although the nurse should be guided by The nurse should first assess connections and tubing to locate the leak while keeping the system functional. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so The priority is Check the tubing and drainage system connections for an air leak, not a step that delays stabilization of circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor pulls attention toward Strip the tubing toward the drainage system, even though the higher-risk finding is The nurse should first assess connections and tubing to locate the leak while keeping the system functional. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because Check the tubing and drainage system connections for an air leak targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first. Nursing safety depends on matching the option to the client's active clinical risk.

questions.takeaway: Continuous water-seal bubbling means assess the system for an air leak before doing anything that could trap air in the chest.

questions.stem: A client with a short leg cast reports increasing pain that is not relieved by opioids. The toes are pale and the client reports numbness. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Notify the provider immediately about possible compartment syndrome.
Apply ice over the cast and reassess after the next pain dose.
Elevate the leg above the level of the heart.
Loosen the cast with scissors at the bedside.

questions.rationale: Pain out of proportion, pallor, and numbness suggest compartment syndrome, which can threaten tissue viability and limb function. Immediate provider notification and urgent evaluation are needed. Comfort-only measures or excessive elevation can worsen ischemia or delay definitive treatment.

distractorRationales.b: Apply ice over the cast and reassess after the next pain dose fits a lower-risk interpretation, despite the clinical warning in numbness suggest compartment syndrome, which can threaten tissue viability and limb function. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so Choosing it can delay medication-safety intervention and leave medication toxicity unresolved.
distractorRationales.c: This choice focuses on Elevate the leg above the level of the heart, despite the clinical warning in Pain out of proportion, pallor. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay medication-safety intervention and leave medication toxicity unresolved.
distractorRationales.d: The clinical error in Loosen the cast with scissors at the bedside is that it anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, and misses that A client with a short leg cast reports increasing pain that is not relieved by opioids. It is less defensible because Notify the provider immediately about possible compartment syndrome targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: Pain that outpaces the injury plus pallor or numbness under a cast should trigger concern for compartment syndrome.

questions.stem: A toddler with croup has a barky cough and now has inspiratory stridor at rest. Which finding most concerns the nurse?

options.text: Stridor at rest with increasing work of breathing.
Hoarse cry that improves when the child rests.
A barking cough that occurs mainly at night.
Mild fever of 100.4 F (38 C).

questions.rationale: Stridor at rest signals worsening upper-airway obstruction and is more serious than a nighttime barky cough or hoarseness alone. The nurse should recognize this as a potential respiratory emergency because increasing work of breathing can quickly progress to fatigue and failure. Mild fever is common and not the most urgent finding here.

distractorRationales.b: This option overweights Hoarse cry that improves when the child rests, instead of responding to Mild fever is common and not the most urgent finding here. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize Stridor at rest with increasing work of breathing; otherwise oxygenation or ventilation remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.c: The clinical error in A barking cough that occurs mainly at night is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, without accounting for Stridor at rest signals worsening upper-airway obstruction and is more serious than a nighttime barky cough or hoarseness. Stridor at rest with increasing work of breathing better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.d: The clinical error in Mild fever of 100.4 F (38 C) is that it normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, without accounting for Stridor at rest signals worsening upper-airway obstruction and is more serious than a nighttime barky cough or hoarseness. Stridor at rest with increasing work of breathing is safer because it addresses oxygenation or ventilation before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: In croup, stridor at rest is the red-flag sign of worsening airway obstruction.
